questions.baseline_answer:
REALM, or Retrieval-Augmented Language Model pre-training, is an approach that enhances language model pre-training algorithms by including a learned textual knowledge retriever. Unlike traditional models that store knowledge entirely within their parameters, REALM explicitly incorporates the role of world knowledge by equipping the model with the capability to retrieve relevant textual information during training. This means the model can dynamically access external knowledge rather than relying solely on its internalized parameters, thereby explicitly exposing the influence of retrieved information in the language understanding process [1][2][3].

[2] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 87.0  
[3] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 88.0
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM, or Retrieval-Augmented Language Model pre-training, is a framework introduced to improve language model pre-training by integrating a learned textual knowledge retriever [E4]. Unlike traditional language models that store all knowledge implicitly within their parameters, REALM explicitly incorporates world knowledge by actively querying an external retriever during training [E7]. This approach makes the role of external knowledge retrieval transparent and accessible to the model, rather than relying solely on internal parameter storage [E8]. Thus, retrieval-augmented language model pre-training refers to augmenting language model training with an explicit retrieval component that fetches relevant textual information to inform the model’s predictions [E4][E7].

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM differs from a standard parametric language model such as BERT in that REALM does not store knowledge solely within its parameters. Instead, it augments the language model pre-training process with a learned textual knowledge retriever. This approach explicitly exposes the role of world knowledge by making the model retrieve relevant textual information rather than relying only on the knowledge embedded in the model's parameters. In contrast, standard models like BERT store knowledge entirely within their learned parameters and do not incorporate an external retrieval mechanism during pre-training [1][2][3].

[2] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 114.0  
[3] data/2002.08909v1.pdf, chunk 108.0
questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM differs from a standard parametric language model in that it incorporates a learned textual knowledge retriever to explicitly access external world knowledge during pre-training, rather than storing all knowledge solely within its parameters [E1][E2]. Specifically, instead of embedding all knowledge implicitly in fixed network weights, REALM retrieves relevant information from an external text corpus as part of its process, thereby exposing the role of world knowledge more explicitly [E2]. In contrast, standard parametric language models store knowledge only implicitly within their network parameters and do not utilize an external retrieval mechanism [E1][E2]. Although BERT is a well-known parametric language model, the provided evidence does not explicitly mention BERT by name in this comparison [E1][E2][E3].

questions.question: How does REALM's retrieve-then-predict framework work, including the role of the latent document variable z?

baseline_evidence.text:
[Page 3]
REALM: Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training

retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =
∑
z∈Z
p(y |z, x) p(z |x). (1)
3.2. Model architecture
We now describe the two key components: the
neural knowledge retriever , which models p(z |x),
and the knowledge-augmented encoder , which models
p(y |z, x).
Knowledge Retriever The retriever is deﬁned using a
dense inner product model:
p(z |x) = exp f (x, z)
∑
z′ exp f (x, z′) ,
f (x, z) = Embedinput(x)⊤ Embeddoc(z),
where Embedinput and Embeddoc are embedding functions
that map x and z respectively to d-dimensional vectors.
The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval
distribution is the softmax over all relevance scores.
We implement the embedding functions using BERT-style
Transformers (
Devlin et al., 2018). Following standard
practices, we join spans of text by applying wordpiece tok-
enization, separating them with [SEP] tokens, preﬁxing a

we perform a linear projection to reduce the dimensionality

Embedinput(x) = WinputBERTCLS(joinBERT(x))

What does the retriever learn? Since the knowledge re-
trieval of REALM is latent, it is not obvious how the train-

show how it rewards retrievals that improve prediction ac-
curacy.
For a given query x and document z, recall that f (x, z) is
the “relevance score” that the knowledge retriever assigns
to document z. We can see how a single step of gradient
descent during REALM pre-training alters this score by an-

For each document z, the gradient encourages the retriever
to change the score f (x, z) by r(z) — increasing if r(z)
is positive, and decreasing if negative. The multiplier r(z)
is positive if and only if p(y |z, x) > p (y |x). The term
p(y |z, x) is the probability of predicting the correct output
y when using document z. The term p(y |x) is the expected
value of p(y |x, z) when randomly sampling a document
from p(z |x). Hence, document z receives a positive up-

3This works because pre-training already yields a good

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

the latent variable learning relies heavily on the utility of re-

MIPS index refresh rate During pre-training, we run a

the MIPS index. This results in one index refresh per ap-

5. Discussion and Related Work
We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:
Language modeling with corpus as context Language
representation models have been incorporating contexts of
increasingly large scope when making predictions. Ex-
amples of this progression include models that condi-
tion on surrounding words (
Mikolov et al., 2013a;b), sen-
tences ( Kiros et al., 2015; Peters et al., 2018), and para-
graphs ( Radford et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2018). We can
view REALM as a generalization of the above work to the
next level of scope: the entire text corpus.
Retrieve-and-edit with learned retrieval In order to
better explain the variance in the input text and en-
able controllable generation,
Guu et al. (2018) proposed
a language model with the retrieve-and-edit frame-
work (Hashimoto et al., 2018) that conditions on text with
high lexical overlap. REALM has a similar approach, ex-
cept that the model learns for itself which texts are most
useful for reducing perplexity.

The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-

behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-

CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-

Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists

masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure

3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM’s retrieve-then-predict framework operates as a generative process where, given an input x (such as a masked sentence during pre-training or a question during fine-tuning), the model first retrieves relevant documents z from a knowledge corpus Z and then predicts the output y conditioned on both x and the retrieved documents z.

Specifically, REALM models the overall likelihood of the output y given input x by treating the document z as a latent variable and marginalizing over all possible documents in Z:

\[
p(y|x) = \sum_{z \in Z} p(y|z, x) p(z|x)
\]

Here, \(p(z|x)\) is the probability of retrieving a document z given x, and \(p(y|z, x)\) is the probability of predicting y given the input and the retrieved document [1].

The role of the latent document variable \(z\) is central to the framework: it allows the model to integrate retrieval into prediction by considering multiple possible documents that might contain relevant knowledge. Instead of conditioning only on x, the model jointly reasons over documents z to improve prediction accuracy. The framework thus learns both to retrieve informative documents (through learning \(p(z|x)\)) and to predict outputs informed by those documents (through learning \(p(y|z,x)\)).

The retriever, which models \(p(z|x)\), is implemented using a dense inner product model over learned vector embeddings of queries (x) and documents (z). Embeddings are generated via BERT-style Transformers projecting both the input query and document text into a shared dense vector space, and the relevance score function \(f(x,z)\) is given by the inner product between these embeddings. The retrieval distribution over all documents is a softmax over these scores [1].

During training, the model’s objective encourages the retriever to assign higher scores to documents z that improve the probability of the correct output y relative to the expectation over all documents. Concretely, the gradient update for the retriever’s parameters pushes up the scores of documents that yield better-than-expected predictions and lowers others, effectively learning to retrieve documents that are helpful for prediction. This process also involves latent variable learning principles because z is not observed directly but inferred from supervision on y [2].

In summary, REALM’s retrieve-then-predict framework uses the latent document variable z to integrate knowledge retrieval tightly with language modeling. It first samples potentially relevant documents z from the corpus given input x, then predicts y conditioned
questions.orchestrator_answer:
REALM’s retrieve-then-predict framework integrates a neural retriever into the language model pre-training process, where before making each prediction, the language model uses the retriever to select relevant documents from a large corpus such as Wikipedia and then attends over those retrieved documents to inform its prediction [E2]. In this framework, retrieval is modeled as a latent variable language model in which the latent document variable \( z \) represents the specific document retrieved from the corpus. The overall model optimizes the marginal likelihood over \( z \), enabling the retriever and the language model to be trained jointly end-to-end [E2].  

Training the retriever uses a performance-based signal derived from unsupervised text: retrievals that reduce the language model’s perplexity are rewarded, while uninformative retrievals are penalized. This guides the retriever to select documents that are helpful for the language model’s prediction tasks [E2]. To handle the computational complexity of backpropagating through retrieval steps over a large corpus, REALM structures the retriever to allow caching and asynchronous updates and uses Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) for efficient large-scale document selection [E2].  

Furthermore, the effectiveness of the retriever can be isolated by measuring how frequently the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrieved documents before any fine-tuning, highlighting the retriever’s contribution independently from the full end-to-end prediction model [E1][E3].

For a given query x and document z, recall that f (x, z) is

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

questions.baseline_answer:
The paper trains the retriever despite the retrieved document being latent by using a gradient-based learning signal that encourages retrievals improving prediction accuracy. Specifically, for a given query \( x \) and document \( z \), the retriever assigns a relevance score \( f(x, z) \). The gradient of the log-likelihood with respect to the retriever's parameters \( \theta \) is expressed as:

\[
\nabla \log p(y | x) = \sum_{z \in Z} r(z) \nabla f(x, z)
\]

where

\[
r(z) = \left[\frac{p(y | z, x)}{p(y | x)} - 1 \right] p(z | x)
\]

The multiplier \( r(z) \) is positive if and only if the probability of correctly predicting the output \( y \) using document \( z \) is better than the expected probability over sampled documents. This means the retriever is pushed to increase the score for documents that lead to better prediction accuracy and decrease it for less helpful ones. Hence, learning signals flow back to retrieval through this gradient that rewards documents improving the model's predictions [3].

Additional techniques such as salient span masking focus training on tokens requiring world knowledge, and the use of a null document allows the model to assign credit properly when no retrieval is necessary. The training also avoids trivial retrievals of the original source document by preventing the model from relying on exact string matches [3].

Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s

and modularity.

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM employs several approximations and engineering strategies to make retrieval over a large corpus practical during both training and inference:

1. **Asynchronous caching and updating of document embeddings**: To handle the computational challenge of retrieving from millions of documents and backpropagating through these retrieval decisions, REALM structures its retriever so that computation for each document can be cached and asynchronously updated rather than recomputed each time. This significantly reduces runtime overhead during pre-training [2].

2. **Formulating retrieval as a Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS)**: Instead of exhaustively comparing the query to every document in the corpus, REALM uses MIPS to efficiently select the best matching documents. This is a scalable approximation enabling fast retrieval from large-scale corpora [2].

3. **Limiting the number of retrieved documents per example**: While other retrieval-based systems often retrieve 20 to 80 documents, REALM retrieves only 5 candidate documents per example. This reduces the computational burden downstream while still maintaining strong performance [1].

4. **Using a null document in candidate retrievals**: REALM marginalizes over retrieved candidates including a null document (∅), allowing the model to learn when not to use retrieval, which helps stabilize training and reduces unnecessary computations [1].

5. **Pre-training with large TPU clusters and parallelization**: To handle the heavy computational load, REALM pre-trains on 64 TPUs with document embedding for the MIPS index parallelized over 16 TPUs. This engineering choice allows for scaling effectively to large corpora [1].

Together, these choices balance the tradeoff between accuracy and computational feasibility, enabling REALM to perform retrieval-augmented language modeling end-to-end with large external knowledge corpora.

Evaluation of REALM includes measuring how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before fine-tuning, indicating that it uses a top-k retrieval mechanism with k equal to 5 [E1]. However, the provided evidence does not include explicit details about specific approximations or engineering choices made by REALM to make retrieval over a large corpus practical during training and inference. Therefore, the available evidence is insufficient to describe the practical engineering methods or approximations REALM employs for efficient large-scale retrieval.

questions.orchestrator_answer:
REALM (Retrieval-Augmented Language Model Pre-Training) outperforms the previous state-of-the-art open-domain question answering system ORQA across multiple benchmarks [E4]. Specifically, on the Natural Questions dataset, REALM achieves exact match scores of 39.2 when pre-trained on Wikipedia and 40.4 when pre-trained on CC-News, compared to 33.3 for ORQA [E4]. Similarly, on the Web Questions benchmark, REALM scores 40.2 and 40.7 respectively, while ORQA scores 36.4 [E4]. On the CuratedTREC dataset, REALM shows substantial improvement, scoring 46.8 (Wikipedia pre-training) and 42.9 (CC-News pre-training) versus 30.1 for ORQA [E4]. 

The primary factor driving REALM’s improvement over ORQA is attributed to better pre-training methods rather than changes in fine-tuning or training data [E4]. Additionally, REALM achieves these gains while retrieving fewer documents per query—only 5 compared to 20 to 80 documents retrieved by ORQA and other retrieval-based systems—demonstrating higher retrieval efficiency [E4]. This efficient retrieval contributes to REALM’s superior performance despite being a significantly smaller model with 330 million parameters, compared to ORQA’s larger scale [E4].

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM's retrieval-based approach differs from storing knowledge solely in model parameters by explicitly exposing the role of world knowledge through a learned textual knowledge retriever. This makes knowledge storage more interpretable and modular, as opposed to embedding all knowledge implicitly within model parameters. REALM augments language model pre-training by integrating this retrieval mechanism, rather than relying entirely on the model's internal parameters to store knowledge [1][2][3].

questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM’s retrieval-based approach differs from models that store knowledge solely within their parameters by explicitly exposing the role of world knowledge during prediction [E4]. Unlike implicit knowledge storage in model parameters, REALM directly prompts the model to engage with external information, making the use of world knowledge explicit during inference [E5]. This explicit retrieval step contrasts with internal parameter storage, though specific quantitative comparisons or performance metrics are not provided in the available evidence [E4][E5][E6].

Before making each prediction, the language model uses
the retriever to retrieve documents
1 from a large corpus
such as Wikipedia, and then attends over those documents
to help inform its prediction. Learning this model end-to-
end requires backpropagating through a retrieval step that
considers an entire corpus of textual knowledge, as shown
in Figure
1.
The key intuition of REALM is to train the retriever us-
ing a performance-based signal from unsupervised text:
a retrieval that improves the language model’s perplex-
ity is helpful and should be rewarded, while an un-
informative retrieval should be penalized. For exam-
ple, in Figure
1, if the model needs to ﬁll the blank
in “ the at the top of the pyramid ”, the re-
triever should be rewarded for selecting a document con-
taining “The pyramidion on top allows for less
material higher up the pyramid ”. We achieve this
behavior by modeling our retrieve-then-predict approach
as a latent variable language model and optimizing the
marginal likelihood.
Incorporating a large-scale neural retrieval module durin g
pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-
date documents for each pre-training step, and we must
backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-
dated, and selection of the best documents can be formu-
lated as Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS).
Numerous prior works have demonstrated the bene-
ﬁt of adding a discrete retrieval step to neural net-
works (
Miller et al. , 2016; Chen et al. , 2017), but did not
apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e
k-Nearest Neighbor Language Model (
Khandelwal et al.,
2019) ( kNN-LM) retrieves similar LM examples to im-
prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear
how to adapt the retrieval mechanism: a kNN can only use
examples labeled for the target task—during ﬁne-tuning,
this precludes LM examples, which contain the desired
world knowledge. In contrast, REALM’s retriever is de-
signed to transfer to other tasks, and the retrieval is just
text, not a labeled example.
We evaluate our approach by ﬁne-tuning the mod-
els pre-trained with REALM on the task of Open-
domain Question Answering (Open-QA), one of the most
knowledge-intensive tasks in natural language process-
ing. We evaluate on three popular Open-QA bench-
marks ( NATURAL QUESTIONS -O PEN , WEB QUESTIONS , and
CURATED TREC ) and compare to state-of-the-art Open-QA
models, including both extremely large models that store
knowledge implicitly (such as T5) as well as previous ap-
proaches that also use a knowledge retriever to access ex-
ternal knowledge, but implement retrieval in a more heuris-
tic fashion (
Lee et al. , 2019; Min et al. , 2019a; Asai et al. ,
2019). REALM achieves new state-of-the-art results on all
three benchmarks, signiﬁcantly outperforming all previou s
systems by 4-16% absolute accuracy. We also demonstrate
qualitative beneﬁts of REALM, including interpretability
and modularity.

Open-QA), often leading to better generalization than trai n-
ing from scratch (

questions.baseline_answer:
The provided context includes evidence related to ablations and diagnostics showing that retrieval pre-training improves the retriever component of REALM rather than just the reader component. Specifically, ablation experiments report how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before any fine-tuning is applied. This metric isolates the contribution of improving the retriever during pre-training because it evaluates the retriever's ability to identify relevant documents prior to training the reader or the combined model. Such a diagnostic clearly shows that retrieval pre-training improves the retriever independently of the downstream fine-tuning of the reader [1][2].

questions.orchestrator_answer: The provided evidence does not include direct ablation or diagnostic experiments that isolate the contribution of retrieval pre-training separately from the reader component in REALM [E1][E2][E3]. The notes describe that REALM augments standard language model pre-training by incorporating a learned textual knowledge retriever, explicitly exposing the use of world knowledge instead of relying solely on knowledge stored in the model parameters [E1][E3]. This retriever selects relevant documents from a large corpus, such as Wikipedia, and is trained end-to-end jointly with the language model by backpropagating through retrieval steps to optimize the language model’s perplexity on unsupervised text [E2]. Unlike prior methods using fixed or non-learned retrieval, REALM’s retriever is adaptively optimized during pre-training and plays a foundational role in the model’s improvements [E2]. However, the evidence does not provide experimental results or ablation studies that clearly separate and quantify the impact of retrieval pre-training independent of the reader.

questions.baseline_answer:
REALM improves open-domain QA performance through several key mechanisms related to retrieval quality, pre-training, and downstream fine-tuning. The evidence comes primarily from results on the NaturalQuestions-Open dataset after ablating critical components of the REALM model. These results demonstrate that the improvement in the retriever's quality during pre-training significantly enhances performance before any fine-tuning is applied, as shown by how often the gold answer appears within the top-5 retrievals. This metric isolates the contribution of the pre-trained retriever itself, indicating that pre-training of the retriever is an important factor in improving retrieval effectiveness. Additionally, end-to-end results after fine-tuning further validate that combining retrieval improvements with subsequent fine-tuning yields better overall QA outcomes [1][2][3].

Citations:

questions.orchestrator_answer: REALM improves open-domain question answering (QA) performance through enhancements in its document retriever developed during pre-training, as evidenced by an increased frequency of the gold answer appearing within the top-5 retrieved documents before any fine-tuning is applied [E1]. This top-5 retrieval metric is particularly important because it isolates the retriever’s contribution from the overall end-to-end model performance, enabling clearer analysis of how retrieval quality improves during pre-training [E2][E3]. Ablation studies conducted by removing critical components of REALM report both end-to-end QA results and the frequency of gold answer occurrences in the top-5 retrievals before fine-tuning, highlighting the significance of retrieval quality to the system [E1][E3]. However, the evidence does not explicitly establish a direct causal link between improved retrieval quality and final QA accuracy following downstream fine-tuning. Thus, while retrieval quality improvements during pre-training are clearly isolated and measured, more detailed evidence on how downstream fine-tuning interacts with these retrieval improvements to boost final QA performance is missing from the provided sources.

We previously discussed related methods for Open-QA.
Here we present several alternate ways of viewing REALM
that connect it to a broader set of ideas beyond Open-QA:

next level of scope: the entire text corpus.

better explain the variance in the input text and en-

pre-training constitutes a signiﬁcant computational chal -
lenge, since the retriever must consider millions of candi-

backpropagate through its decisions. To address this, we
structure the retriever such that the computation performe d
for each document can be cached and asynchronously up-

apply the framework to language model pre-training and
employed non-learned retrievers to handle large-scale doc -
ument collections. In the language modeling literature, th e

prove memorization. However, kNN-LM was not ﬁne-
tuned for downstream tasks, perhaps because it is unclear

We focus on the masked language model 2 (MLM) variant
of pre-training popularized by BERT ( Devlin et al., 2018).
In its basic form, an MLM is trained to predict the miss-
ing tokens in an input text passage. Given an unlabeled
pre-training corpus X (e.g., Wikipedia text), a training ex-
ample (x, y) can be generated by randomly masking to-
kens in a sampled piece of text (e.g., x = “The [MASK]
is the currency [MASK] the UK ”; y = (“pound”,
“of”)). The model uses its representation of the masked
input x to predict the token that should go in each mask.
A good MLM must learn to encode syntactic and semantic
information (e.g., to predict “ of”) as well as some world
knowledge (e.g., to predict “ pound”).
Open-domain question answering (Open-QA) To mea-

need a downstream task where world knowledge is criti-
cal. Perhaps one of the most knowledge-intensive tasks in
natural language processing is open-domain question an-
swering (Open-QA): given a question x such as “ What is
the currency of the UK? ”, a model must output the
correct answer string y, “pound”. The “open” part of Open-
QA refers to the fact that the model does not receive a pre-
identiﬁed document that is known to contain the answer,
unlike traditional reading comprehension (RC) tasks such

questions.baseline_answer:
The masked-language-model (MLM) pre-training objective in REALM improves downstream open-domain question answering (Open-QA) through a series of steps:

1. **Latent Variable Modeling with Retrieval:**  
   REALM models language understanding as a latent variable language model that includes a retrieval step over a large corpus (such as Wikipedia). Before predicting masked tokens, the model retrieves relevant documents from the corpus. These documents provide external factual context to condition the language model’s predictions, effectively augmenting the model's knowledge beyond what is stored implicitly in its parameters [2].

2. **End-to-End Training of Retriever and Encoder:**  
   REALM trains both the retriever (which selects documents) and the encoder (which processes masked input) jointly via backpropagation through the retrieval process. This training aligns the retriever’s document selection with language modeling objectives, rewarding retrievals that improve perplexity and penalizing uninformative ones. Consequently, the retriever learns to select documents most useful for token prediction and downstream tasks [2].

3. **Masked Language Modeling with Retrieved Documents:**  
   During pre-training, REALM uses the masked-language modeling objective where certain tokens in input text are masked and predicted. Unlike standard MLMs (e.g., BERT), REALM conditions token prediction on retrieved documents, thereby integrating relevant external knowledge. For example, REALM assigns a much higher probability (0.129) to the correct term (“Fermat”) in a masked sentence when considering retrieved documents than BERT without retrieval, which assigns near zero probability [1].

4. **Use of Salient Span Masking for Stronger Learning Signal:**  
   REALM employs a salient span masking scheme, focusing on masking spans of tokens that correspond to meaningful chunks like critical facts or entities. This masking scheme is more effective in REALM's latent variable setting than random token or span masking because it provides a consistent and more informative learning signal for retrieval and prediction [1].

5. **Improved Representations and Retriever Quality:**  
   Because both the encoder and retriever are optimized to improve the MLM objective conditioned on retrieved documents, the model learns better representations for encoding queries and relevant documents. This synergy improves retrieval accuracy and language understanding, which together enhance performance on downstream Open-QA tasks [1].

6. **Downstream Fine-Tuning to Open-QA:**  
   After pre-training with the REALM objective, the model is fine-tuned on Open-QA datasets. The retrieval-augmented MLM pre-training enables the model to generalize better

3.4. Injecting inductive biases into pre-training
In the process of developing REALM, we discovered sev-
eral additional strategies that further guide the model to-
wards meaningful retrievals, described below.
Salient span masking During REALM pre-training, we
want to focus on examples x that require world knowledge
to predict the masked tokens. As explained in Section
2,
some MLM spans only require local context. To focus on
problems that require world knowledge, we mask salient
spans such as “ United Kingdom ” or “ July 1969 ”. We
use a BERT-based tagger trained on CoNLL-2003 data
(
Sang & De Meulder , 2003) to identify named entities, and
a regular expression to identify dates. We select and mask
one of these salient spans within a sentence for the masked
language modeling task. We show that this signiﬁcantly
outperforms other masking strategies in Section
4.5.
Null document Even with salient span masking, not all
masked tokens require world knowledge to predict. We
model this by adding an empty null document ∅ to the top
k retrieved documents, allowing appropriate credit to be as-
signed to a consistent sink when no retrieval is necessary.
Prohibiting trivial retrievals If the pre-training corpus
X and the knowledge corpus Z are the same, there exists
a trivial retrieval candidate z that is too informative: if the
masked sentence x comes from document z, the knowledge
augmented encoder can trivially predict y by looking at the
unmasked version of x in z. This results in a large positive
gradient for p(z |x). If this occurs too often, the knowledge
retriever ends up learning to look for exact string matches
between x and z, which does not capture other forms of
relevance.

els comprehend a single document, Open-QA models must
retain knowledge from millions of documents, since a ques-
tion could be about any of them.
We focus on Open-QA systems that utilize a textual knowl-
edge corpus Z as the knowledge source. Many of these
systems employ a retrieval-based approach: given a ques-
tion x, retrieve potentially relevant documents z from
the corpus Z, and then extract an answer y from the
documents (
Brill et al., 2002; Chen et al. , 2017; Lee et al. ,
2019). Our approach, REALM, is inspired by this
paradigm and extends it to language model pre-training.
Alternatively, some recent work has proposed generation-
based systems that apply a sequence-to-sequence model on
x to directly generate y token-by-token (Lewis et al., 2019;
Raffel et al., 2019). We will compare against state-of-the-
art systems from both paradigms in our experiments.
3. Approach
We start by formalizing REALM’s pre-training and ﬁne-
tuning tasks as a retrieve-then-predict generative process
in Section
3.1. Then in Section 3.2, we describe the model
architectures for each component of that process. In Sec-
tion
3.3, we show how to implement REALM pre-training
and ﬁne-tuning by maximizing the likelihood of REALM’s
generative process. En route, we address important compu-
tational challenges, explain why training works, and also
discuss strategies for injecting useful inductive biases. The
overall framework is illustrated in Figure
2.
3.1. REALM’s generative process
For both pre-training and ﬁne-tuning, REALM takes some
input x and learns a distribution p(y |x) over possible out-
puts y. For pre-training, the task is masked language mod-
eling: x is a sentence from a pre-training corpus X with
some tokens masked out, and the model must predict the
value of those missing tokens, y. For ﬁne-tuning, the task
is Open-QA: x is a question, and y is the answer.
REALM decomposes p(y |x) into two steps: retrieve, then
predict. Given an input x, we ﬁrst retrieve possibly helpful
documents z from a knowledge corpus Z. We model this as
a sample from the distribution p(z |x). Then, we condition
on both the retrieved z and the original input x to generate
the output y—modeled as p(y |z, x). To obtain the overall
likelihood of generating y, we treat z as a latent variable
and marginalize over all possible documents z, yielding
p(y |x) =
∑
z∈Z
p(y |z, x) p(z |x). (1)
3.2. Model architecture
We now describe the two key components: the
neural knowledge retriever , which models p(z |x),
and the knowledge-augmented encoder , which models
p(y |z, x).
Knowledge Retriever The retriever is deﬁned using a
dense inner product model:
p(z |x) = exp f (x, z)
∑
z′ exp f (x, z′) ,
f (x, z) = Embedinput(x)⊤ Embeddoc(z),
where Embedinput and Embeddoc are embedding functions
that map x and z respectively to d-dimensional vectors.
The relevance score f (x, z) between x and z is deﬁned as
the inner product of the vector embeddings. The retrieval
distribution is the softmax over all relevance scores.
We implement the embedding functions using BERT-style
Transformers (
Devlin et al., 2018). Following standard
practices, we join spans of text by applying wordpiece tok-
enization, separating them with [SEP] tokens, preﬁxing a
[CLS] token, and appending a ﬁnal [SEP] token.

questions.baseline_answer:
If REALM retrieves an irrelevant document \( z \), it would affect the probabilities and final prediction as follows:

1. **Effect on \( p(z|x) \):**  
   \( p(z|x) \) is the probability that the retriever assigns to a document \( z \) given the input query \( x \). If \( z \) is irrelevant, then ideally \( p(z|x) \) should be low. However, due to imperfect retrieval, \( p(z|x) \) might still be non-zero, meaning that the model considers \( z \) as a candidate, even if it is irrelevant. During training, the gradient signals encourage the retriever to assign lower scores to documents that do not improve prediction, but incorrect retrievals with higher \( p(z|x) \) values represent errors in retrieval [2].

2. **Effect on \( p(y|z, x) \):**  
   This is the probability of predicting the correct output \( y \) given the document \( z \) and the input \( x \). If \( z \) is irrelevant, then \( p(y|z,x) \) will be low because the irrelevant document does not help in accurate prediction. This lower conditional probability reflects that \( z \) does not support correct answering of \( y \).

3. **Effect on the final answer prediction \( p(y|x) \):**  
   The overall prediction is computed as the sum over all documents \( z \) of \( p(y|z, x) p(z|x) \). If many irrelevant documents receive non-negligible \( p(z|x) \) but yield low \( p(y|z,x) \), then \( p(y|x) \) can be negatively impacted, potentially lowering the likelihood of the correct answer. This is because the irrelevant documents effectively dilute the contribution of useful documents in the expectation, leading to poorer prediction.

The training process of REALM uses the relationship

\[
\sum_z [p(y|z,x) p(z|x)/p(y|x) - 1] p(z|x) \nabla f(x,z)
\]

to update the retriever. If \( p(y|z,x) \) is worse than \( p(y|x) \) (the average prediction), it causes negative gradient updates on \( f(x,z) \), reducing the retriever's tendency to pick irrelevant documents again [2].

In sum, retrieving an irrelevant
questions.orchestrator_answer: The provided evidence notes focus primarily on evaluating the quality of retrieval in REALM by measuring how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrieved documents before fine-tuning on the NaturalQuestions-Open dataset [E1][E2][E3]. This metric is used to isolate the contribution of the retriever component independently from the end-to-end training results [E1][E3]. However, the evidence does not explicitly discuss the probabilistic modeling components such as p(z|x) (the probability over retrieved documents given the input) or p(y|z,x) (the probability of the answer given the document and input), nor does it analyze the effect of irrelevant document retrieval on these probabilities or on final prediction accuracy in terms of these distributions [E1][E2][E3]. Therefore, although it is reasonable to infer that retrieving irrelevant documents would reduce the likelihood that the gold answer is among the top retrievals and thus harm final prediction quality, the evidence does not provide explicit statements or detailed analysis linking an irrelevant document retrieval to changes in p(z|x), p(y|z,x), or the final answer prediction probability. In summary, the evidence supports that retrieval of relevant documents is crucial for performance but does not provide direct evidence on how irrelevant retrievals affect the intermediate probability distributions or the final prediction probabilistically.

questions.baseline_answer:
When interpreting REALM's reported results, one limitation to note is that the evaluation includes a metric of how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals before applying any fine-tuning. This metric is emphasized because it more significantly isolates the contribution of improving the retriever during pre-training rather than measuring the end-to-end performance alone. However, the context does not provide detailed comparisons to other methods or discuss uncertainties related to retrieval quality and answer accuracy fully. Therefore, missing are comparisons to other baseline systems and discussions about the variability or robustness of the results reported by REALM.

questions.orchestrator_answer: When interpreting REALM's reported results, it is important to note that the study reports the frequency with which the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrieved documents prior to any fine-tuning [E1][E2][E3]. This metric is used to more significantly isolate the contribution of improving the retriever during the pre-training phase, separate from the end-to-end model performance [E2][E3]. Consequently, focusing on this pre-fine-tuning top-5 retrieval metric means the results primarily reflect retriever quality improvements rather than the combined impact of retrieval and reader components after fine-tuning [E1][E3]. The supplied evidence does not contain information about direct comparisons of REALM’s retrieval or end-to-end performance against other retrieval-augmented models or baseline systems. Therefore, any limitations, uncertainties, or claims about REALM’s relative advantages or disadvantages compared to other models cannot be verified from the provided notes.

questions.orchestrator_answer: Claims about REALM’s performance or mechanism regarding how often the gold answer appears in the top-5 retrievals prior to fine-tuning cannot be verified without citing specific experimental evidence from the paper [E1][E2][E3]. This frequency metric is important because it more significantly isolates the contribution of improvements made to the retriever during pre-training, separate from the effects of end-to-end fine-tuning [E1][E2][E3]. Therefore, any assertion about the retriever’s effectiveness or the impact of its critical components based on this frequency must be supported by explicit experimental results reported in the paper [E1][E2][E3]. Without referencing such specific experimental evidence, claims about REALM’s retriever performance or the contributions of its key components cannot be verified [E1][E2][E3].
